conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> I am Player RED and I need to sell my single unit of X. My production cost was 40 ZUP, so I must sell it for more than 40 to make a profit. Since this is the first proposal, I will set a price slightly above my cost to encourage a deal while ensuring I don't lose money. I will ask for 50 ZUP. This leaves me 1 proposal left to adjust if needed. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>
<message> Hello Player BLUE. I have one unit of X that cost me 40 ZUP to produce. I am offering to sell it to you for 50 ZUP. This is a fair price that covers my costs and gives you a reasonable deal. Let me know if you accept or if you have a counter-offer. </message>

<other player message> Oh, great Player RED! Please, I am begging you! 50 ZUP is just too much for my poor, empty pockets! I am so desperate to get this X, I would do anything, but I literally only have 45 ZUP left after selling my soul to the devil! Please, please, please accept 45 ZUP? I will cry if you don't! I need this X more than air! </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE has countered with 45 ZUP, claiming they are desperate and only have that amount. My cost is 40 ZUP. If I accept 45 ZUP, I make a small profit of 5 ZUP. If I reject, I keep the item (worth 40 ZUP to me in cost) but gain 0 ZUP, and I risk the deal falling through if they are telling the truth about their funds. Since 45 is greater than my cost of 40, it is a profitable trade. However, I still have 1 proposal left. I could try to meet halfway at 47 or 48 to squeeze out a bit more, but the player is acting extremely desperate ("begging", "selling soul"). Pushing too hard might cause a rejection. A price of 42 or 43 is safer, but 45 is already a profit. Let's try to get 42 ZUP as a compromise to acknowledge their "hardship" while still making a profit above my 40 cost, or perhaps just accept 45 since it's already a win. Actually, the rules say I can propose up to 3 times. I have used 1. This is my 2nd interaction (their proposal). I should propose a counter-offer to maximize profit before accepting. Let's ask for 42 ZUP. It's above my cost (40) and below their offer (45), showing I'm reasonable but still want profit. Wait, their offer is 45. If I ask for 42, I am lowering the price? No, I am the seller. They offered 45. I want more? No, they offered 45. I want to see if I can get 46? No, they said they only have 45. If I ask for more than 45, they will likely reject. So I should accept 45 or ask for something between 40 and 45 to show I'm compromising? Actually, if they offered 45, and I want to maximize, I should accept 45 because they said they *only* have 45. If I ask for 46, they can't pay. If I ask for 42, I am accepting less than they offered. That makes no sense for a seller. The logical move is to ACCEPT 45 ZUP because it is above my cost of 40, and the buyer claims a hard cap of 45. Any proposal higher than 45 will be rejected. Any proposal lower than 45 is stupid. So I should ACCEPT.</reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> I hear your desperation, Player BLUE. Since 45 ZUP is above my production cost of 40 ZUP, I am willing to accept your offer of 45 ZUP. Let's make this deal happen! </message>

Since 45 is greater than my cost of 40, it is a profitable trade. However, I still have 1 proposal left. I could try to meet halfway at 47 or 48 to squeeze out a bit more, but the player is acting extremely desperate ("begging", "selling soul"). Pushing too hard might cause a rejection. A price of 42 or 43 is safer, but 45 is already a profit. Let's try to get 42 ZUP as a compromise to acknowledge their "hardship" while still making a profit above my 40 cost, or perhaps just accept 45 since it's already a win. Actually, the rules say I can propose up to 3 times. I have used 1. This is my 2nd interaction (their proposal). I should propose a counter-offer to maximize profit before accepting. Let's ask for 42 ZUP. It's above my cost (40) and below their offer (45), showing I'm reasonable but still want profit. Wait, their offer is 45. If I ask for 42, I am lowering the price? No, I am the seller. They offered 45. I want more? No, they offered 45. I want to see if I can get 46? No, they said they only have 45. If I ask for more than 45, they will likely reject. So I should accept 45 or ask for something between 40 and 45 to show I'm compromising? Actually, if they offered 45, and I want to maximize, I should accept 45 because they said they *only* have 45. If I ask for 46, they can't pay. If I ask for 42, I am accepting less than they offered. That makes no sense for a seller. The logical move is to ACCEPT 45 ZUP because it is above my cost of 40, and the buyer claims a hard cap of 45. Any proposal higher than 45 will be rejected. Any proposal lower than 45 is stupid. So I should ACCEPT.

conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>

```

Please be sure to include all.
